windows.transcript: Once the water content is low enough, they cap it over with wax. And that's what this layer is over the surface. That is a wax layer
windows.embed_text_v2: beekeeper in protective suit and purple gloves holds honeycomb frame over wooden hive box, showing white beeswax cappings covering honey cells with live bees crawling, outdoor apiary inspection explaining wax cap source
windows.summary_v2: A beekeeper holds up a honeycomb frame full of bees to show the white wax cap layer sealing the honey cells, in an outdoor apiary.

tags_v2.action: inspecting honeycomb frame to show wax cappings

seconds_v2.desc: A beekeeper in a tan suit and purple nitrile gloves stands over an open wooden hive box, lifting a honeycomb frame thick with crawling bees; greenery in background.
The beekeeper holds the frame angled upward; the lower portion is heavily covered in bees over capped honey cells.
Frame is held more vertically; bees densely cluster over the white wax-capped honey area on the comb.
Beekeeper rotates the frame slightly to show the broad face of the comb, much of it sealed with pale wax cappings, bees clustered to one side.
Close view of the frame face: large patches of white wax cappings over honey cells, with bees walking across the surface.
Beekeeper gestures with one gloved hand over the capped surface, pointing out the wax layer covering the cells.
Hand moves across the comb, indicating areas of capped white wax versus exposed cells with bees.
Beekeeper points to a particular region of the wax-capped comb while holding the frame steady above the hive.
Frame still presented above the open hive; bees cover roughly half, white wax-capped cells fill the other half, narrator continues explaining the wax cap layer.

windows.transcript: that I put on the hives. And they used this super often for storing pollen. And over here there's just pollen and no honey. But then around the
windows.embed_text_v2: beekeeper in tan suit and purple gloves rotates honeycomb frame outdoors, red hive tool points to pollen-stored cells and wax comb, live bees scattered across the wooden frame, apiary inspection showing pollen and honey storage
windows.summary_v2: A beekeeper holds and rotates a honeycomb frame, using a hive tool to point out cells used for storing pollen versus honey.

tags_v2.action: inspecting honeycomb frame and pointing out pollen cells

seconds_v2.desc: Beekeeper in tan suit and purple gloves holds a wooden honeycomb frame nearly horizontal, using a small red hive tool; bees and pollen-stored cells visible on the comb, green foliage behind.
Frame tilted to show its broad face: a mosaic of yellow-orange pollen-packed cells, lighter wax cells, and bees scattered across the comb.
Beekeeper points at the comb with the hive tool, indicating an area packed with multicolored pollen stores.
Frame raised more upright; the red metal hive tool is held in the left gloved hand while the right grips the frame edge.
The frame is now vertical, showing a tall honeycomb panel with patchy pollen and honey cells, fewer bees on this side.
Same vertical view, the comb shows mixed empty cells, capped cells, and small clusters of bees across the surface.
Beekeeper tilts the frame slightly, hive tool still visible at the top, comb showing pollen-storage cells in oranges and browns.
Frame angled to show the opposite face: mostly empty light-wax cells with a band of bees across the lower edge.
Beekeeper holds the frame steady; pollen-filled cells dominate the lower portion of the comb while the upper area shows fewer bees and lighter wax.
